amr: Amr is a common name among early Muslim narrators, borne by several Companions and Successors. The most prominent include Amr ibn al-As (may Allah be pleased with him), the renowned Companion, military commander, and conqueror of Egypt, and Amr ibn Dinar, a leading Successor scholar of Mecca. Without additional context, this entry refers to one of several narrators of this name who transmitted hadith in the early Islamic tradition.
al-mughira-bin-shuba: Al-Mughira bin Shu'ba (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. He was a skilled diplomat and military leader, serving as governor of Basra and Kufa during the caliphates of Umar and Uthman. He narrated numerous hadiths and was known for his intelligence and political acumen. He passed away around 50 AH.
abu-salama: Abu Salama ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was a leading Successor scholar of Medina and one of the seven great jurists of the city. He was the son of the Companion Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf (may Allah be pleased with him). Abu Salama transmitted extensively from Aisha, Abu Huraira, and other Companions, making him a key link in numerous hadith chains. He was highly regarded for his piety and legal knowledge.
rafi-bin-khadij: Rafi bin Khadij (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in several battles and is known for narrating hadiths on agricultural contracts, particularly the prohibition of certain share-cropping arrangements. He was a reliable narrator whose reports are found in the major hadith collections. He lived until approximately 74 AH.
abu-is-haq: Abu Is-haq al-Sabi'i (Amr ibn Abdullah al-Hamdani) was a prominent Successor scholar from Kufa who lived through the era of the senior Companions. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, al-Bara ibn Azib, and Zaid ibn Arqam (may Allah be pleased with them). He was considered a highly reliable narrator in his earlier years and transmitted a large number of hadiths. He passed away around 127 AH.
abu-said-al-khudri-ra: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him) was a distinguished Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in twelve battles alongside the Prophet and narrated over one thousand hadiths, making him among the most prolific Companion narrators. He was particularly known for his hadiths on Islamic jurisprudence, eschatology, and ethical conduct. He passed away in Medina around 74 AH.
tawus: Tawus ibn Kaysan al-Yamani was a leading Successor scholar and jurist from Yemen. He narrated from numerous Companions including Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with them), and was considered among the most learned men of his generation. He was known for his asceticism, refusal of gifts from rulers, and independent legal opinions. He passed away in Mecca around 106 AH during the Hajj season.
muawiya-ibn-abi-sufyan: Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca. He served as a scribe of the Quranic revelation and later became the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate, ruling for nearly twenty years. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and is regarded as a Companion within Ahl us-Sunnah, though his political role remains a subject of historical discussion. He passed away in 60 AH.
zaid-bin-khalid-al-juhani: Zaid bin Khalid al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Juhayna. He participated in the Battle of Hudaybiyyah and is particularly known for narrating hadiths related to found property (luqata), purification, and prayer. He settled in Medina and later Kufa, where he transmitted hadith until his passing around 68 AH. His reports appear in all major hadith collections.
abdullah-bin-az-zubair: Abdullah bin Az-Zubair (may Allah be pleased with him) was the son of the Companion Al-Zubair ibn al-Awwam and the grandson of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. He was the first child born to the Muslim emigrants (Muhajirun) in Medina after the Hijra, and his birth was considered a blessed sign for the community. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from senior Companions, and later became the Caliph in Mecca. He was martyred in 73 AH.
an-numan-bin-bashir: An-Nu'man bin Bashir (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad whose father Bashir ibn Sa'd was also a Companion. He was brought to the Prophet as an infant, making him among the youngest Companions. He later served as governor of Kufa and Homs under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan. He is renowned for narrating the famous hadith on the halal and haram being clear and the importance of avoiding doubtful matters. He was martyred around 65 AH.
abu-hazim: Abu Hazim al-Ashja'i (Salmaan, the freed slave of Azda) was a Successor who transmitted hadiths from several Companions. A second narrator of the same name, Abu Hazim ibn Dinar al-Araj, was a notable Medinian Successor scholar who narrated extensively from Abu Huraira and Sahl ibn Sa'd. Both were reliable transmitters whose reports appear in the Sahihayn and Sunan collections. The name Abu Hazim was borne by several trustworthy scholars of the early generations.
salama-bin-al-akwa: Salama bin Al-Akwa (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad renowned for his exceptional speed and skill in foot-racing. He participated in numerous battles, including Hudaybiyyah, Khaybar, and Hunayn, and gave his pledge of allegiance under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He narrated many hadiths related to warfare, hunting, and the Prophet's conduct. In his later years he settled in the Rabadha region and passed away around 74 AH.
abu-huraira-rad: Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with him) was the most prolific narrator of hadith among all the Companions of the Prophet Muhammad, transmitting over five thousand reports. He embraced Islam in 7 AH and remained closely attached to the Prophet, dedicating himself to memorizing his sayings. He served as governor of Bahrain and later settled in Medina, where he taught thousands of students. He passed away around 57-59 AH. This entry is a variant spelling of the same Companion.
abubakrah: Abu Bakrah Nufay ibn al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who came to Islam during the Siege of Ta'if, descending from the fortress to join the Muslims. He settled in Basra, where he became one of the leading Companions in that region and a respected transmitter of hadith. He is well known for narrating the Prophet's prohibition against fighting in civil strife. He passed away in Basra around 51-52 AH.
abdullah-ibn-abbas: Abdullah ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him) was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions. The Prophet supplicated for him, asking Allah to grant him deep understanding of religion and knowledge of the Quran's interpretation. He became the foremost authority on Quranic exegesis (tafsir) and Islamic jurisprudence, earning the title Hibr al-Umma (Scholar of the Nation) and Turjuman al-Quran (Interpreter of the Quran). He narrated over one thousand six hundred hadiths and passed away in Ta'if around 68 AH.
ibn-shihab: Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri (Muhammad ibn Muslim ibn Shihab) was one of the most important Successor scholars in Islamic history, considered a master of hadith science and one of the earliest systematic collectors of the Prophet's traditions. He narrated from numerous Companions including Anas ibn Malik and Sahl ibn Sa'd, and served as a teacher to Imam Malik and many other leading scholars. He was among the first to formally compile hadiths on the instruction of Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz. He passed away around 124 AH.
qatadah: Qatadah ibn Di'ama al-Sadusi was a prominent Successor scholar from Basra, renowned as a memorizer of the Quran and an authority in hadith and Quranic exegesis. Despite being blind from birth, he possessed an extraordinary memory and narrated from Anas ibn Malik, Abdullah ibn Sarjas, and other Companions. He was a student of al-Hasan al-Basri and a leading representative of the Basran scholarly tradition. He passed away around 117 AH.
abu-uthman: Abu Uthman al-Nahdi (Abd al-Rahman ibn Mall) was a senior Successor scholar who lived an exceptionally long life, reportedly reaching 130 years of age. He narrated from several Companions including Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, Salman al-Farsi, and Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with them). He was based in Basra and was considered a highly reliable and trustworthy narrator. He passed away around 95-100 AH.
salama: Salama is a name shared by several early Muslim narrators. The most notable is Salama bin Al-Akwa (may Allah be pleased with him), the Companion renowned for his bravery and speed in battle. Another is Salama ibn Kuhail, a Successor scholar from Kufa who narrated from several Companions and Successors and was considered reliable by hadith critics. Without further context, this entry refers to one of the several narrators named Salama in the early Islamic tradition.
as-saib-bin-yazid: As-Sa'ib bin Yazid (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who was brought to the Prophet as a child during the Farewell Pilgrimage. He is among the Companions who lived long enough to narrate directly from the Prophet and remain a link to the prophetic era well into the Umayyad period. He narrated hadiths related to weights, measures, and commerce in Medina, and is known for his accounts of the market of Medina. He passed away around 91 AH.
um-atiyya: Um Atiyya al-Ansariyyah (Nusayba bint al-Harith, may Allah be pleased with her) was a female Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Medina. She participated in seven military expeditions, providing medical care and support to the wounded. She narrated hadiths related to the washing of the deceased, funeral rites, and women's religious practice, and her reports are particularly important in Islamic jurisprudence. She is among the most cited female Companions in the hadith literature.
al-bara-bin-azib: Al-Bara bin Azib (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in approximately fifteen battles alongside the Prophet and narrated a significant number of hadiths. He is known for narrating the hadith describing the Prophet's full ablution in detail, as well as reports related to prayer direction, funeral rites, and the questioning of the deceased in the grave. He settled in Kufa and passed away around 71-72 AH.
ibn-abi-mulaika: Ibn Abi Mulaika (Abdullah ibn Ubaydullah ibn Abi Mulaika al-Tamimi) was a Successor scholar and judge from Mecca who served as a judge during the caliphate of Ibn al-Zubair. He narrated extensively from Aisha, Ibn Abbas, and other senior Companions (may Allah be pleased with them), and was considered a highly reliable narrator by hadith scholars. He is known for transmitting many hadiths on Islamic law, ethics, and the conduct of the Prophet. He passed away around 117 AH.
samurah: Samurah ibn Jundab (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a number of hadiths. He served as governor of Basra under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan and was known as an administrator. He narrated hadiths on prayer, commercial transactions, and prophetic supplications. His reports appear in the Sunan collections. This entry may overlap with Samurah bin Jundab listed separately; both refer to the same Companion.
abu-masud-al-ansari: Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (Uqba ibn Amr al-Badri, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in the Battle of Badr, which gave him his well-known epithet. He narrated numerous hadiths and later settled in Kufa, where he became a respected religious authority. He is known for transmitting the famous hadith on the connection between the prayer and charity (zakat) and is recognized as a reliable narrator in all major collections.
al-miswar-bin-makhrama: Al-Miswar bin Makhrama (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad, born in Mecca two years before the Hijra. He was the nephew of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf and narrated hadiths from the Prophet, Abu Bakr, and Umar (may Allah be pleased with them). He is known for his narrations related to the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and the Prophet's conduct in diplomacy. He died at the time of Ibn al-Zubair's siege of Mecca around 64 AH.
zaid-bin-arqam: Zaid bin Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina who participated in seventeen military expeditions. He is known for narrating the famous hadith of Ghadir Khumm in which the Prophet emphasized the importance of the Quran and his household. He transmitted many hadiths on theology, prayer, and the Prophet's virtues, and settled in Kufa where he was held in high esteem. He passed away around 65-68 AH.
said-bin-al-musaiyab: Sa'id bin Al-Musaiyab was one of the seven great jurists of Medina and the foremost Successor scholar of his generation. His father and grandfather were Companions of the Prophet. He narrated extensively from Abu Huraira, whose daughter he married, as well as from Umar, Uthman, Ali, and Aisha (may Allah be pleased with them). He was renowned for his legal acumen, his refusal to accept gifts from rulers, and his profound piety. He passed away around 94 AH.
abu-saeed: Abu Sa'eed is likely a variant reference to Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (may Allah be pleased with him), the prolific Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He narrated over one thousand hadiths and was a key transmitter of prophetic traditions on jurisprudence, eschatology, and ethics. Alternatively, this may refer to another narrator sharing this kunya. His reports are found in all major hadith collections and are highly relied upon by Islamic scholars.
abuumamah: Abu Umamah al-Bahili (Sudayy ibn Ajlan, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who was the last surviving Companion in Syria. He narrated a large number of hadiths and was renowned for his frequent recitation of the Quran. He settled in Syria and became a leading religious authority there. He is known for hadiths encouraging the recitation of Surah al-Baqarah to ward off evil, and for numerous narrations on worship and virtue. He passed away around 86-88 AH.
abumusa-al-ashari: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari (Abdullah ibn Qays, may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Yemen. He was a renowned reciter of the Quran, and the Prophet praised his beautiful voice, saying it was like a flute of the family of David. He served as governor of Basra and Kufa and played a significant diplomatic role. He narrated many hadiths on prayer, recitation, and Islamic conduct. He passed away around 42-52 AH.
abuddarda: Abu al-Darda (Uwaymir ibn Zayd, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina, known for his wisdom, asceticism, and deep knowledge of religion. He embraced Islam after the Battle of Badr and became one of the learned scholars among the Companions. He served as a judge in Damascus and was sent by Caliph Uthman to teach the people of Syria. He narrated many hadiths on wisdom, piety, and spiritual conduct. He passed away around 32 AH.
asma: Asma is a name shared by several female Companions and early Muslim women. The most prominent is Asma bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), daughter of the first Caliph Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, who earned the title Dhat al-Nitaqayn (the one of the two belts) for her role during the Hijra. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet and from her father, and lived to nearly one hundred years. Another is Asma bint Yazid, a Companion who narrated from the Prophet on matters affecting women.
abu-umamah: Abu Umamah al-Bahili (Sudayy ibn Ajlan, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who became the last surviving Companion in the Levant region. He narrated a significant number of hadiths and was well known for his dedication to Quranic recitation and religious teaching in Syria. He is cited in the Sunan and Musnad collections for hadiths on worship, ethics, and the virtues of specific deeds. He passed away around 86-88 AH. This entry is a variant of AbuUmamah.
al-hasan: Al-Hasan al-Basri (al-Hasan ibn Abi al-Hasan Yasar) was one of the greatest Successor scholars and spiritual figures in Islamic history. Born in Medina in 21 AH, he narrated from numerous Companions including Anas ibn Malik, Abu Huraira, and Aisha (may Allah be pleased with them). He became the leading scholar of Basra, renowned for his eloquence, profound piety, and asceticism. He was a major transmitter of hadiths and a foundational figure in Islamic spirituality and ethics. He passed away in 110 AH.
uqbah-bin-amir: Uqbah bin Amir al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who served as governor of Egypt under Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan. He was skilled in archery and the Prophet encouraged his practice of it. He narrated numerous hadiths on prayer, charity, and the virtues of Islamic practice, and is known for preserving traditions related to archery and horsemanship. He passed away in Egypt around 58 AH. This entry may overlap with Uqba bin Amir listed separately.
amr-bin-dinar: Amr bin Dinar al-Makki was a prominent Successor scholar and jurist from Mecca. He narrated from the Companions Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, Jabir ibn Abdullah, and others (may Allah be pleased with them), and was one of the principal teachers of Imam al-Shafi'i's teacher, Ibn Uyayna. He was considered among the most reliable and widely respected narrators of his generation. His hadiths appear prominently in the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections. He passed away around 126 AH.
ibn-juraij: Ibn Juraij (Abd al-Malik ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Juraij) was a major Successor scholar from Mecca and one of the early systematizers of hadith collection. He was among the first scholars to compile hadiths in written form and narrated from Ata ibn Abi Rabah, Amr ibn Dinar, and Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri among others. He is considered a foundational figure in the development of Islamic jurisprudence in the Meccan school. He passed away around 150 AH.
uqba-bin-amir: Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad known for his knowledge of the Quran, skill in archery, and dedication to the path of Islam. He served as governor of Egypt and transmitted many hadiths on prayer, purification, charity, and the importance of archery in Islamic practice. He is also known for narrating the Prophet's words on the obligations of Muslims toward one another. He passed away in Egypt around 58 AH.
aisha-rad: Aisha bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her) was the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the greatest scholars of Islam. She narrated over two thousand hadiths and was a primary source of knowledge about the Prophet's private life, worship, and character. After the Prophet's passing, she became a leading teacher and jurist, with Companions and Successors traveling to consult her on religious matters. She passed away in Medina in 58 AH at approximately sixty-six years of age.
al-mughirah-ibn-shubah: Al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a distinguished Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam before the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. He was a gifted diplomat and military commander who served as governor of Basra and Kufa. He narrated numerous hadiths and was known for his intelligence and political skill. He is cited in the major hadith collections for reports on prayer, the Sunnah, and the conduct of the Prophet. He passed away around 50 AH. This is a variant spelling of Al-Mughira bin Shu'ba.
an-numan-ibn-bashir: An-Nu'man ibn Bashir (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the youngest among them. His father Bashir ibn Sa'd was a Companion who participated in the Battle of Badr, and he was brought to the Prophet as an infant. He later served as governor of Kufa and Homs. He is most famous for narrating the foundational hadith: 'The halal is clear and the haram is clear, and between them are doubtful matters.' He was martyred around 65 AH. This is a variant spelling of An-Nu'man bin Bashir.
wail-ibn-hujr: Wa'il ibn Hujr (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from a noble Hadrami family in Yemen. He was received warmly by the Prophet, who gave him land and sent Companions to accompany him back to Yemen. He narrated hadiths primarily on the physical description of the Prophet's prayer, including the placement of hands and the raising of the finger in tashahhud, which are foundational for Islamic prayer practice. He settled in Kufa and his descendants became notable scholars.
kab-bin-malik: Ka'b bin Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a renowned Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina and one of the three Companions who remained behind from the Battle of Tabuk. His account of repentance and Allah's acceptance, narrated at length in Sahih al-Bukhari, is one of the most detailed stories in the hadith literature. He was also a poet who defended the Prophet through verse. He narrated many hadiths and passed away around 50 AH.
humaid: Humaid al-Tawil (Humaid ibn Abi Humaid) was a Successor scholar from Basra who narrated extensively from Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him), to whom he had direct access for many years. He is among the most important transmitters of Anas's hadiths and his narrations appear frequently in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He was considered reliable and trustworthy by hadith scholars. He passed away around 142-143 AH.
abdullah-bin-dinar: Abdullah bin Dinar was a Successor scholar from Medina who was a freed slave (mawla) of Abdullah ibn Umar (may Allah be pleased with him). He narrated extensively from Ibn Umar, whose household he served, and his hadiths appear prominently in the Sahihayn. He was considered a highly reliable narrator by the scholars of hadith criticism and was a key transmitter of Medinan traditions. He passed away around 127 AH.
abu-said-al-khudri-and-abu-huraira: This entry refers to hadiths narrated jointly or within the same chain by two prominent Companions: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and Abu Huraira (may Allah be pleased with them both). Abu Sa'id was an Ansari Companion who narrated over one thousand hadiths, while Abu Huraira was the most prolific Companion narrator with over five thousand hadiths. When their names appear together, the hadith typically has a combined or parallel chain from both authorities, strengthening its authenticity.
asma-bint-abu-bakr: Asma bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first Caliph. She earned the renowned title Dhat al-Nitaqayn (She of the Two Belts) for splitting her belt to provide food for the Prophet and her father during their migration to Medina. She narrated hadiths from the Prophet and lived to nearly one hundred years, witnessing the era of her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubair. She passed away in 73 AH.
jarir-bin-abdullah: Jarir bin Abdullah al-Bajali (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam in 10 AH, near the end of the prophetic era. The Prophet praised his handsome appearance and his goodness. He was sent by the Prophet to destroy the idol Dhu al-Khalasa in Yemen. He settled in Kufa and narrated many hadiths, including the famous tradition on the obligation to advise and show sincerity to Muslim leaders. He passed away around 51-54 AH.
abdullah-bin-abi-qatada: Abdullah bin Abi Qatada was a Successor scholar and the son of the Companion Abu Qatada al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him), one of the Prophet's horsemen known for his bravery in battle. Abdullah narrated hadiths from his father and other Companions, transmitting traditions related to prayer, fasting, and the conduct of the Prophet. His reports serve as an important link between the Companion generation and the Successor scholars.
abdullah-bin-mughaffal: Abdullah bin Mughaffal al-Muzani (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the distinguished members of the Ahl al-Suffa, the poor Companions who lived in the mosque of Medina. He participated in the conquest of Mecca and was among those who carried the banner during battles. He narrated hadiths on prayer, purification, and Islamic etiquette, and later settled in Basra. He passed away around 57-60 AH.
uqba-bin-al-harith: Uqba bin Al-Harith (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Mecca who embraced Islam at the conquest. He narrated a relatively small number of hadiths, among the most notable being a report related to the prohibition of intoxicants following an incident where a man claimed a certain woman had suckled him and the man he sought to marry her to. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are cited in matters of Islamic family law.
abdullah-bin-masud: Abdullah ibn Masud (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the most learned Companions of the Prophet Muhammad and the sixth person to embrace Islam. He served as the Prophet's personal attendant and was allowed to enter upon him at all times. He was renowned as a master of Quranic recitation and Islamic jurisprudence and became the primary religious authority for the people of Kufa. He narrated approximately eight hundred hadiths and passed away in Medina around 32-33 AH.
jabir-bin-abdullah-ra: Jabir bin Abdullah al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Medina who participated in nineteen military expeditions. His father Abdullah ibn Amr was martyred at Uhud. Jabir narrated approximately one thousand five hundred hadiths and is known for detailed accounts of the Prophet's Farewell Pilgrimage, acts of worship, and daily conduct. He passed away in Medina around 73-78 AH and was the last surviving Companion in that city.
jabir-bin-samurah: Jabir bin Samurah (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad, the son of Samurah ibn Jundab and nephew of Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas. He narrated approximately one hundred and forty hadiths and is known particularly for his narrations describing the physical appearance of the Prophet, accounts of Friday prayer, and the signs of the Hour. He settled in Kufa and was considered a reliable narrator. He passed away around 74 AH.
salman-al-farsi: Salman al-Farsi (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who had journeyed from Persia in search of the true religion, passing through Christianity before reaching Islam. The Prophet said about him: 'Salman is from us, the People of the House.' He suggested digging the trench during the Battle of the Trench, an innovative military strategy that proved decisive. He served as governor of al-Mada'in and was known for his profound knowledge and asceticism. He passed away around 36 AH.
ubadah-ibn-as-samit: Ubadah ibn as-Samit (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina and one of the twelve representatives (nuqaba) selected at the Second Pledge of Aqaba. He participated in the Battle of Badr and transmitted important hadiths on the conditions of the shahada, the obligation of prayer, and the prohibition of usury. He served as a judge in Palestine and narrated hadiths that form foundational principles in Islamic jurisprudence. He passed away around 34 AH.
buraidah: Buraidah ibn al-Husayb al-Aslami (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Aslam. He embraced Islam when the Prophet passed through his territory during the Hijra to Medina. He narrated numerous hadiths on jihad, prayer, and the conduct of the Prophet, and his reports are found in all major hadith collections. He settled in Marw (in present-day Turkmenistan) where he became a religious authority and passed away around 62-63 AH.
rafi-ibn-khadij: Rafi ibn Khadij (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina who participated in numerous battles. He is particularly known for narrating hadiths related to agricultural share-cropping arrangements and the conditions under which they are permissible or prohibited. His reports are fundamental in Islamic commercial law. He lived until approximately 74 AH. This entry is a variant spelling of Rafi bin Khadij.
samurah-bin-jundab: Samurah bin Jundab (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a number of hadiths on prayer, dreams, and prophetic conduct. He served as governor of Basra during the caliphate of Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan and was known as an administrator. He is cited in the major hadith collections for traditions including the Prophet's account of a long dream describing the states of various people in the afterlife. He passed away around 58-60 AH.
abu-qilaba: Abu Qilaba al-Jarmi (Abdullah ibn Zayd al-Azdi) was a prominent Successor scholar from Basra who narrated from Anas ibn Malik, Malik ibn al-Huwayrith, and other Companions. He was known for his strong adherence to the Sunnah and his caution in narrating hadiths. He eventually relocated to Syria to distance himself from the political turbulence of his era. He is cited in the Sahihayn and Sunan for hadiths on prayer, purification, and Islamic conduct. He passed away around 104-107 AH.
hakim-bin-hizam: Hakim bin Hizam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who had the distinction of being born inside the Ka'ba, making him the only person in history known to have been born in that sacred place. He was the nephew of Khadijah, the Prophet's first wife. He embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca and went on to perform extensive acts of charity and freeing of slaves. He narrated hadiths on commerce and generosity and passed away around 54-60 AH at a very advanced age.
ibn-abu-mulaika: Ibn Abu Mulaika (Abdullah ibn Ubaydullah ibn Abi Mulaika) was a Successor scholar from Mecca who served as a judge under Caliph Ibn al-Zubair and as a muezzin and prayer leader. He narrated from Aisha, Ibn Abbas, Ibn Umar, and other senior Companions (may Allah be pleased with them), and was regarded as a highly reliable narrator. His hadiths appear prominently in the Sahihayn. He passed away around 117 AH. This entry is a variant spelling of Ibn Abi Mulaika.
ibn-abi-aufa: Ibn Abi Aufa (Abdullah ibn Abi Aufa al-Aslami, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the Battle of Hudaybiyyah and numerous other expeditions. He was among the last Companions to pass away in Kufa, living to a very advanced age, and was considered the last surviving Companion in that city. He narrated hadiths on prayer, charity, and the Prophet's conduct in battle. He passed away around 86-87 AH.
abdullah-bin-abi-aufa: Abdullah bin Abi Aufa al-Aslami (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the Battle of Hudaybiyyah and gave the pledge under the tree. He settled in Kufa, where he became the last surviving Companion in the city, living well into old age. He narrated hadiths on prayer, war, and the conduct of the Prophet, and his reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He passed away around 86-87 AH. This is a fuller version of the Ibn Abi Aufa entry.
ibn-masud-ra: Abdullah ibn Masud (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the most eminent Companions of the Prophet Muhammad and among the earliest to embrace Islam. He was the Prophet's close personal attendant, trusted to carry his sandals and prepare his bath. He was a master of Quranic recitation and is known for his own mushaf. He became the primary religious scholar and judge in Kufa, where thousands of students learned from him. He narrated approximately eight hundred hadiths. He passed away around 32-33 AH.
anas-bin-malik-ra: Anas bin Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion who served the Prophet Muhammad for ten years in Medina, beginning when he was about ten years old. He was the personal servant of the Prophet and narrated over two thousand hadiths, making him one of the most prolific Companion narrators. He was the last surviving Companion in Basra, where he settled and taught for decades. His narrations cover virtually every aspect of the Prophet's daily life and practice. He passed away around 91-93 AH.
zayd-ibn-arqam: Zayd ibn Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina who participated in seventeen military expeditions. He is renowned for narrating the hadith of Ghadir Khumm in which the Prophet spoke about the Quran and his household. He transmitted many hadiths on theology, prayer, and the Companions' merits, and settled in Kufa. He passed away around 65-68 AH. This is a variant spelling of Zaid bin Arqam.
anas-b-malik: Anas b. Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was among the most prolific narrators of hadith among the Companions, having served the Prophet Muhammad personally for ten years in Medina. He was entrusted by his mother Umm Sulaym to serve the Prophet when he arrived in Medina. His narrations provide intimate accounts of the Prophet's habits, worship, character, and daily life. He was the last Companion to pass away in Basra and died around 91-93 AH. This entry is a variant form of Anas bin Malik.
ubayy-ibn-kab: Ubayy ibn Ka'b (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the foremost Companions of the Prophet Muhammad in Quranic knowledge and recitation. He was one of the four Companions whom the Prophet specifically instructed his followers to learn the Quran from. He served as a scribe of the Quranic revelation and was the Imam of the Muslims in Medina in Tarawih prayer during the caliphate of Umar. He narrated hadiths on the Quran, worship, and the Prophet's character. He passed away around 19-30 AH.
aishah-wife-of-prophet: Aisha bint Abi Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), wife of the Prophet Muhammad, was one of the greatest scholars and narrators in Islamic history. She narrated over two thousand hadiths and was the primary authority on the Prophet's private worship, domestic life, and personal conduct. Companions and Successors frequently consulted her on religious rulings and she issued fatwas on matters affecting women and the broader Muslim community. She passed away in Medina in 58 AH. This entry is an alternate descriptive form of Aisha.
abu-rafi: Abu Rafi (may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who served as his freed slave and close attendant. He is known for narrating hadiths about the Prophet's personal habits, household conduct, and acts of worship. He is sometimes confused with other narrators sharing the same kunya. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are cited in matters of Islamic law and the Prophet's character. He settled in Medina and narrated traditions to the Successor generation.
sad-ibn-abuwaqqas: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (may Allah be pleased with him) was one of the ten Companions given the glad tidings of Paradise and among the earliest to embrace Islam, reportedly the third or sixth person to do so. He was a maternal uncle of the Prophet Muhammad and a skilled archer. He led the Muslim army in the decisive Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, completing the conquest of Persia, and became the first to shed blood in the cause of Islam. He narrated many hadiths and passed away around 55-58 AH.
ibrahim: Ibrahim al-Nakha'i was a leading Successor scholar and jurist from Kufa, considered one of the most important authorities in the Kufan legal school that later developed into the Hanafi madhhab. He narrated from his aunt Aisha, from Anas ibn Malik, and from senior Successor scholars. He was the primary teacher of Hammad ibn Abi Sulayman, who in turn taught Imam Abu Hanifa. He was known for his deep legal knowledge and cautious approach to hadith narration. He passed away around 95-96 AH.
jabir-b-abd-allah: Jabir b. Abd Allah al-Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him) was a prominent Companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Khazraj tribe of Medina. He participated in many battles and narrated approximately one thousand five hundred hadiths covering worship, pilgrimage, commerce, and the Prophet's character. His detailed account of the Farewell Pilgrimage is among the most comprehensive preserved descriptions of the rites of Hajj. He passed away in Medina around 73-78 AH. This is a variant form of Jabir bin Abdullah.
ikrimah: Ikrimah was a prominent Successor scholar and the freed slave (mawla) of Abdullah ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him). He was considered the foremost student of Ibn Abbas in Quranic exegesis and hadith and narrated an enormous number of traditions from him. He traveled widely to transmit knowledge across the Islamic world and his narrations appear in all major hadith collections. While some early scholars debated his reliability on account of reported Kharijite sympathies, the majority of hadith scholars accepted his narrations. He passed away around 104-107 AH.
jundub: Jundub ibn Abdullah al-Bajali (or al-Azdi, may Allah be pleased with him) was a Companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in several battles. He is known for narrating hadiths related to the prohibition of sorcery and the obligation to act upon one's faith. He is also reported to have executed a sorcerer, which became a point of discussion among jurists regarding the legal ruling on magic practitioners. His reports appear in the major hadith collections. He passed away in the early Umayyad period.
